content_en:
The personal status law section covers marriage, divorce, kinship, and related family matters — the domain of Islamic law most directly encountered by ordinary Muslim families. Dr. al-Zuhayli's treatment is particularly valuable here because personal status law is the area where the four schools differ most significantly and where contemporary Muslim communities most need reliable guidance.

On marriage, the author covers the conditions and pillars of a valid nikah contract across the four schools. One of the most practically significant differences is on the requirement of the wali (guardian): Hanafi law permits a legally competent adult woman to contract her own marriage without a wali, while the Maliki, Shafi'i, and Hanbali schools require the wali as a condition of validity. Dr. al-Zuhayli presents the evidence — primarily the hadith 'there is no nikah without a wali' — and explains the Hanafi counter-argument based on the principle that a woman with full legal capacity may dispose of her own affairs. This difference has major implications for Muslim communities navigating family situations in non-Muslim countries.

The mahr (dower) section covers its types, the rules for its payment, the difference between prompt (mu'ajjal) and deferred (mu'ajjal) mahr, and what happens to the mahr in cases of divorce before and after consummation. The prohibition on the bride's family receiving or consuming the mahr is emphasized, as this practice — common in some cultures — contradicts the Quranic injunction to give women their mahr as a gift (4:4).

On divorce, the encyclopedia presents the schools' positions on the triple divorce pronounced in a single sitting — a topic of enormous practical consequence. The Hanafi, Maliki, and Shafi'i schools generally treat it as counting as three, while Ibn Taymiyyah and his school held it counts as one. Dr. al-Zuhayli presents both views carefully and objectively. The chapter also covers ila' (oath of abstinence), zihar (injurious comparison), li'an (mutual imprecation), and khul' — the complete landscape of Islamic marriage dissolution.
